pattern: Bitwise AND only ever stays the same or loses bits as you fold in more numbers, so the cheapest possible total is bounded below by zero the moment a running AND hits zero -- greedily cutting there whenever possible maximizes how many pieces you get without ever paying more than the unavoidable minimum.
hints: AND-ing more numbers together can never raise the result, only keep it the same or clear more bits -- so once a running AND reaches zero it can never do better than zero no matter what else you fold in.

Walk the array left to right keeping a running AND of the current piece. The instant that running AND becomes zero, that piece is already as cheap as any piece can be (zero), so close it off right there, start a fresh running AND, and count one more piece.

If the running AND of the whole array never once reaches zero along the way, the cheapest possible total is just the AND of everything, and that can only be achieved by keeping the entire array as a single piece -- so the answer collapses to exactly one subarray in that case.

edge_inputs.description: single element that is already zero
every element already zero, each can stand alone
no subarray AND ever reaches zero
two elements each at the maximum allowed value
zero only reachable by combining all three elements together
a trailing nonzero element after two zero-producing runs
shares no bits pairwise but never individually zero on its own

stress.description: large array with zeros sprinkled in often enough to force many cuts
large array of mostly small values sharing many bits, few or no zeros
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 15) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
